title: Australian GP Reactions: Belacik's Mixed Verdict, Vandesteene's Stunning Upset
caption: Wesley Vandesteene — the surprise hero of Melbourne.
body:
Twenty-four hours on from Ferrari's lights-to-flag victory in Melbourne, the paddock remains in a peculiar state of divided conversation. Väisänen's win was clinical and commanding, but it's what happened around him that has left the broader grid reeling. The Australian Grand Prix did not simply crown a winner — it exposed vulnerabilities, created opportunities, and fired a shot across the bow of the championship contenders.

Belacik: Harsh Verdict, But Not Without Hope

Ferrari Team Principal Randy Belacik did not mince words in his assessment of Afonso Cashinho's Melbourne weekend. "A P17 qualify and a P12 finish — those results are not acceptable for a Ferrari driver," Belacik told reporters. "That said, this was Friday for him. One race in difficult conditions against the best in the grid. We know what he can do. I've seen his pace, I've seen his racecraft. Shanghai will be a different story."

Belacik's balance of criticism and conviction was notable. While he made clear that results must improve immediately, his tone suggested not a driver whose seat is under threat, but one being tested — and perhaps one the team still believes in. Cashinho was handed the keys to Liam Dela Cruz's seat, a driver long treated as irreplaceable. That pressure is immense, but Belacik's carefully chosen words hinted that the team's faith has not evaporated after one weekend. "We didn't bring him here to fail," Belacik said. "We brought him here because we think he's the right fit for the future. Melbourne was a referendum on whether he belongs. Shanghai will be the real answer."

Vandesteene: "The Gamble Paid Off"

If Cashinho was the talking point for wrong reasons, Wesley Vandesteene was the talking point for all the right ones. Mercedes' call to react aggressively at the safety car — committing early and emerging in second position — is already being described as one of the most decisive single-lap tactical calls the team has made in years. Vandesteene, starting P5, drove the restart and the entire second half with composure well beyond his race experience, keeping his nerve under pressure from a charging field to bring home a P2 finish that Mercedes could barely have dreamed of pre-race.

"I trusted what the team told me, and they gave me the car in a position to win," Vandesteene said. "In those conditions you just drive what's in front of you. I'm very happy with this result but I know there is still a great deal more to come from me this season."

The result means Mercedes lead the Constructors' Championship after the opening round — 28 points to Ferrari's 25. Two cars consistently in the points beats one dominant win and one zero without question.

MaraDöner: A Podium That Shook the Paddock

One of the two Greek drivers on the 2026 grid, Michiii Maradöner, took a moment on the Melbourne podium that will live long in the paddock's memory. Michiii MaraDöner, who spent much of pre-season being discussed as a raw talent still finding his feet in the series — produced a fully mature P3 drive that stifled almost every sceptical conversation overnight. Ascending from P8, managing the wet chaos after Kondra's crash, and holding composure when the safety car reshuffled the pack, MaraDöner was clinical in a race that punished drivers who were anything less.

Alpine haven't announced the result with much fanfare, mindful of not loading a rookie with excessive expectation. But behind closed doors, the Alpine engineering group is reportedly delighted. The car, already capable without being outstanding, now has a driver who can extract a podium from adversity on his early race weekends in the series.

Väisänen: Ice-Cool and Already the Benchmark

Eetu Väisänen was asked whether winning his first race of the season in dominant fashion placed pressure on him to repeat it in Shanghai. His answer was characteristically brief. "We won. The car was quick. We'll go again." For a driver who controlled almost every lap of a treacherous, chaotic race, he has earned the right to be economical with words. Ferrari's first win places them as the early form team, and behind Väisänen's composed exterior, his engineers will already be studying China's very different compound and surface requirements.

Springfield, Kevu, and the Wide Points Picture

Sarah Springfield's P6 for Aston Martin emerged as one of the under-reported stories of the weekend. Starting P18, the Aston Martin driver worked through the wet confusion methodically and brought home six points that will look very meaningful if the car is as competitive as it appeared in Melbourne's specific conditions. Quarter Kevu, P7 and fastest lap, left Melbourne knowing he was undone by the puncture at the worst possible moment — but also knowing that five points from a chaotic, wet season opener gives him a launchpad for Shanghai. The championship's first race rarely defines it. What comes next will.
